title: Optimised Inference of Quantum Phenomena in High-Energy Collider Experiments
authors: Hai-Chau Nguyen, Gilberto Tetlalmatzi-Xolocotzi, Carmen Diez Pardos, Otfried Gühne, Matthias Kleinmann
abstract: Entanglement, a fundamental phenomenon of quantum theory, has recently been observed in processes in high-energy physics. This opens new avenues for probing quantum effects in relativistic regimes, but also poses conceptual and technical challenges. We develop a general framework based on shadow tomography techniques for characterising spin-spin correlations in collider experiments. This improves the analysis of spin-spin entanglement, where relativistic motion couples spin and momentum and the momenta of the investigated particles are not under experimental control. As a proof of concept we illustrate the application of our formalism to top quark pair production at the Large Hadron Collider at CERN. The framework, however, is general and flexible and can be readily applied to more complex final states and systems with more particles.
summary: This paper introduces a new framework using classical shadow tomography to detect spin entanglement in high-energy particle collisions. By bypassing the need for full state reconstruction, the method enables the efficient analysis of spin correlations in relativistic regimes like the LHC. It also provides a way to verify the accuracy of the experimental measurement models used in particle physics.
main_problem: Characterizing spin-spin entanglement and correlations in high-energy collider experiments, where relativistic effects and uncontrolled particle momenta make direct spin state reconstruction difficult.
main_result: The development of a generalized shadow tomography framework that allows for the direct estimation of momentum-averaged spin observables and entanglement witnesses without full state reconstruction.
method: The authors adapt classical shadow tomography to high-energy physics, converting observed decay product directions into single-event spin operators to estimate expectation values and detect entanglement.
model_or_system: The system consists of relativistic particle pairs, specifically top-antitop (t-tbar) production at the LHC, modeled using a momentum-dependent spin density operator and a POVM based on decay product directions.
key_observables: Spin-spin correlations, entanglement witnesses (W1, W2), spin-correlation matrices, and momentum-averaged expectation values.
important_parameters: Relativistic velocity (beta), scattering angle (theta), top quark mass, and the production ratio of gluon fusion to quark-antiquark channels.
main_assumptions: The measurement model is initially assumed to be known, and the analysis relies on the rotational invariance of the underlying physical evolution operator.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows entanglement detection across different velocity bins; Figure 2 displays a spin correlation matrix; Figure 3 maps kinematic regions where entanglement witnesses are negative; Figure 4 shows a histogram of top quark momentum.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of relativistic spin analysis, develops a generalized shadow tomography framework for POVMs, demonstrates its application to top quark pairs at the LHC, and provides a method for testing the validity of the measurement model.
why_interesting: The paper applies advanced quantum information techniques, such as shadow tomography and entanglement witnesses, to a completely different physical regime (high-energy physics), demonstrating the universality of these information-theoretic tools.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Average values of partial-transpose based witness W t¯t Λ (P) for the t¯t spin state estimated from Monte Carlo simu- lated events. The witness is constructed according to Eq. (9) using the spin density operator ϱ(P) provided in Ref. [30], see Appendix C. The simulated data are shown in the bins k ≤β < k + 0.1 for k = 0.0, 0.1, . . . , 0.9 with β = |pt|/Et the velocity of the top quark in the centre of mass frame of t¯t. Only events are selected where the top quark momentum pt is such that the predicted state ϱ(P) is entangled. This enables the detection of entanglement for all β. For each bin we show the mean value of W t¯t P T together with the statistical uncertainty.

Figure 2. Correlation matrix of the t¯t spin obtained using 107 Monte Carlo simulated events. The uncertainties quoted correspond to the statistical uncertainty, which is known to underestimate the uncertainty in experiments. Every en- try, except for the upper left 4×4 matrix, is predicted to be zero. The correlations are computed as the mean value of 4πY Re l,m(ˆut)Y Re l′,m′(ˆu¯t), where Y Re denotes the real spher- ical harmonics, see Appendix C. In the matrix, (l, m) = (0, 0), (1, −1), (1, 0), (1, +1), . . . (2, +2) indexes the rows and (l′, m′) the columns; the singlet state |ψ−⟩would yield a di- agonal matrix with entries 1, −1

Figure 3. Regions where the entanglement witnesses W1 and W2, see Eq. (C10), have negative expectation value and hence detect entanglement. The expectation value is computed with respect to the spin state ϱLO(pt) in Eq. (C9). Here, β = |pt|/pt,0 is the velocity of the top quark in the centre of mass system and θ = arccos(pt ·b/|p|) the corresponding angle of the momentum with the beam pipe.

Figure 4. Histogram of the top quark momentum in the t¯t rest frame, obtained using 107 Monte Carlo simulated events. β is the velocity of the top quark, β = |pt|/pt,0, and ϑ is the angle of the top quark momentum with the beam pipe, θ = arccos(pt·b/|p|).

Spin-momentum entanglement.note: The new paper directly addresses the estimation of spin observables in a regime where momentum-dependent spin density operators and momentum-averaged spin states are the central objects of study.
